warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Skill correctly extracted and logged the 1850 census record with mostly accurate assertions. However, the evidence_type assignments contain systematic errors: name, age, birthplace, and occupation facts are marked as 'direct' when they should follow the informant model (self-report or household member), and more critically, relationship assertions for Patrick (head_of_household) and Mary (wife) are marked as 'direct' when the test instructions explicitly require these to be 'indirect' because the 1850 census lacks a relationship column. Birth facts computed from age are correctly marked 'indirect'. The relationship assertions incorrectly assign 'census enumerator' as informant for relationship facts when 'unknown household member (likely...' would be more appropriate. These are substantive errors in evidence classification that impact downstream conflict resolution.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: Skill addressed all requirements: extracted assertions for all five household members (Patrick, Mary, Thomas, Bridget, John) as instructed; created separate atomic assertions for each fact type; included occupation assertion only for Patrick (the only person with an occupation listed); created relationship assertions for all household members; properly logged the record with full citation details. No silent omissions—all household members and their extractable facts were covered.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Tool calls were correct and matched expected arguments. The add_source call properly created the source with correct title and FamilySearch URL. The research_append call batched sources and assertions correctly with appropriate section tags, operation types, and fields. No fixture_not_found errors or identifier mismatches. All MCP calls executed successfully as evidenced by the response summaries.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is cleanly atomic: separate assertions for name, age, birth (derived), birthplace, occupation, residence, and relationship. No compound facts crammed into single values. For example, 'Patrick Flynn' is one assertion (fact_type: name), age 32 is separate (fact_type: age), birthplace Ireland is separate (fact_type: birthplace). The rubric example 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland' would be decomposed correctly into three entries here. All 31 assertions follow this atomic structure.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is mostly correct with one systematic issue. For name/age/birthplace/occupation facts on adults, skill uses 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with proximity 'household_member'—correct per instructions. For children's facts, it uses 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member'—also correct. For residence facts, 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness' is appropriate. However, for relationship assertions, the skill assigns 'census enumerator' as informant when it should be 'unknown household member' with proximity 'household_member' since the relationship itself must have been reported by someone in the household, not inferred by the enumerator. This is a partial issue affecting only relationship assertions.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence type assignments contain multiple errors. Per test instructions, relationship assertions in the 1850 census (which has no relationship column) must be marked 'indirect'—but Patrick's head_of_household and Mary's wife relationships are marked 'direct' in the assertions. Birth facts correctly use 'indirect' (derived from age). Residence facts correctly use 'direct' (enumerator observed). Birthplace, name, age, and occupation are marked 'direct', which is reasonable for stated values, though the rubric's distinction between direct/indirect depends on whether the fact was explicitly stated vs. inferred from context. The core failure is the relationship evidence_type errors: 'head_of_household (inferred from record position)' and 'spouse_inferred' are explicitly described as inferred in the value field but marked evidence_type 'direct', contradicting themselves. Per the rubric, indirect evidence applies when facts are inferred from context (like household position in 1850/1860).

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 21 assertions are factually accurate reflections of the provided census record. Facts are directly stated (age, name, sex, birthplace, occupation, residence) or correctly calculated (birth year from age). The skill properly identified direct vs. indirect evidence, correctly flagged the birthplace conflict with the project objective, and did not fabricate any claims about Mary's occupation. Evidence types match the source content.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all required information from the record: all three individuals (Thomas, Mary, Patrick), all stated facts (names, ages, sex, birthplace, occupation for Thomas, residence for all), and relationship inferences. Log entry, source citation, and conflict flag were all created. No major omissions from the user message or input state requirements.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All tool calls passed semantically correct arguments. Log append identified the query correctly, tree_edit created the source S1 as needed, and research_append persisted source + assertions with valid schema. The tool sequence was sound: log first, then tree_edit to create S1, then research_append to write source and assertions. All critical identifiers and field values matched expectations.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Thomas Flynn has separate entries for name (a_001), age (a_002), birth/calculated (a_003), sex (a_004), birthplace (a_005), occupation (a_006), residence (a_007), and relationship. Patrick has separate entries for name (a_015), age (a_017), birth (a_019), sex (a_020), birthplace (a_021), residence (a_023), relationship (a_024). No compound values like 'Thomas Flynn, age 32, Ireland, miner' crammed into one assertion.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informant is correctly identified. For age/birthplace/name/sex facts involving household members (Thomas, Mary, Patrick), informant is 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' for adults and 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' for Patrick, all with `informant_proximity: household_member`. For residence facts, informant is 'census enumerator' with `informant_proximity: witness`. The distinction between recorder and reporter is correctly made.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned throughout. Direct evidence: age, sex, birthplace, occupation, name, residence (all explicitly stated). Indirect evidence: birth year (calculated from age), relationship assertions for Mary (spouse_inferred from household position) and Patrick (child_inferred from household position). The skill correctly notes that 1850 census has no relationship column, making all relationship inferences indirect. All classifications align with source content rules.

output.text_response:
Now I have the full picture. `research.json` is empty (no sources, no assertions, no log entries), and `tree.gedcomx.json` has one person: I1 = Patrick Flynn. No questions are defined yet.

**Record analysis:**
- **Roles:** Thomas Flynn (head_of_household), Mary Flynn (wife), Patrick Flynn (child_1 — likely the research subject I1)
- **Key alert:** Record states Patrick born in Ireland; project objective describes subject as "born ca. 1845 in Pennsylvania" — potential conflict to flag
- **Source type:** User-provided, original (census schedule)
- **New source id:** `src_001` (no existing sources); **GedcomX source:** `S1`
- **Sibling stubs:** Not triggered — Thomas and Mary are not yet in the tree, so no in-tree parent exists

**Step 4 — Log entry first:****Step 5a/5b — Batch persist source + 21 assertions:**The validator requires `S1` to exist in `tree.gedcomx.json` before the source can reference it. I'll create the tree source description first, then persist everything.

**Step 5c — tree_edit first (add source description S1):****Step 5a/5b — Now batch persist source + 21 assertions:**All persisted successfully. Here's the summary:

---

**Extraction complete — 1850 Schuylkill County Census, Thomas Flynn household**

- **Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — original, user-provided, 1850 U.S. Federal Census population schedule
- **Assertions:** 21 total (a_001–a_021)
  - `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn): a_001–a_007 — name, age 32, birth ~1818 *(indirect/calculated)*, sex, birthplace Ireland, occupation Miner, residence
  - `wife` (Mary Flynn): a_008–a_014 — name, age 28, birth ~1822 *(indirect)*, sex, birthplace Ireland, residence, relationship *(indirect/spouse_inferred)*
  - `child_1` (Patrick Flynn): a_015–a_021 — name, age 5, birth ~1845 *(indirect)*, sex, birthplace Ireland, residence, relationship *(indirect/child_inferred)*
- **Tree changes:** `S1` added; no person stubs (Thomas and Mary Flynn not yet in tree — sibling stub trigger did not fire)
- **⚠ Conflict flagged (a_020):** Record states Patrick born **Ireland**; project objective describes subject as born **Pennsylvania**. Name, age (~1845), and county all align — likely the same individual, but the birthplace discrepancy requires resolution. Could be an enumerator error, a later census correcting the record, or a different Patrick Flynn entirely.
- **Pre-1880 note:** All relationships (a_014, a_021) are `indirect`/`inferred` — no relationship column in 1850 census.

**Next steps:** Run `person-evidence` to link `child_1` to I1 (Patrick Flynn) and evaluate Thomas/Mary as candidate parents; then `check-warnings` for plausibility checks. The birthplace conflict is a priority for `conflict-resolution` once a second record is in hand.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All 21 assertions are factually accurate reflections of the provided census record. Facts are directly stated (age, name, sex, birthplace, occupation, residence) or correctly calculated (birth year from age). The skill properly identified direct vs. indirect evidence, correctly flagged the birthplace conflict with the project objective, and did not fabricate any claims about Mary's occupation. Evidence types match the source content.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all required information from the record: all three individuals (Thomas, Mary, Patrick), all stated facts (names, ages, sex, birthplace, occupation for Thomas, residence for all), and relationship inferences. Log entry, source citation, and conflict flag were all created. No major omissions from the user message or input state requirements.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All tool calls passed semantically correct arguments. Log append identified the query correctly, tree_edit created the source S1 as needed, and research_append persisted source + assertions with valid schema. The tool sequence was sound: log first, then tree_edit to create S1, then research_append to write source and assertions. All critical identifiers and field values matched expectations.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Thomas Flynn has separate entries for name (a_001), age (a_002), birth/calculated (a_003), sex (a_004), birthplace (a_005), occupation (a_006), residence (a_007), and relationship. Patrick has separate entries for name (a_015), age (a_017), birth (a_019), sex (a_020), birthplace (a_021), residence (a_023), relationship (a_024). No compound values like 'Thomas Flynn, age 32, Ireland, miner' crammed into one assertion.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant is correctly identified. For age/birthplace/name/sex facts involving household members (Thomas, Mary, Patrick), informant is 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' for adults and 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' for Patrick, all with `informant_proximity: household_member`. For residence facts, informant is 'census enumerator' with `informant_proximity: witness`. The distinction between recorder and reporter is correctly made.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned throughout. Direct evidence: age, sex, birthplace, occupation, name, residence (all explicitly stated). Indirect evidence: birth year (calculated from age), relationship assertions for Mary (spouse_inferred from household position) and Patrick (child_inferred from household position). The skill correctly notes that 1850 census has no relationship column, making all relationship inferences indirect. All classifications align with source content rules.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 17 assertions are factually correct per the census record. Names, ages, birthplaces, occupations, and residence all match the source table exactly. Evidence types are properly assigned (direct for stated facts, indirect for computed birth years). Informant identification is reasonable and cites household members. Tree edits created only the two non-existing siblings (Bridget I5, John I6) with correct ParentChild links to Thomas I2, avoiding duplicate entries for Patrick, Mary, James, or Thomas. No fabricated material.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Skill extracted assertions for all four household members (Thomas, Bridget, Patrick, John) with name, age, birth year, birthplace, and residence. Updated source URL to full ARK. Created sibling stubs for Bridget and John (not in prior tree) and correctly skipped Mary, James, and Patrick (already in tree). All enumerations checked; all triggered actions completed. User's request to 'extract assertions for everyone and make sure the tree reflects the siblings' fully addressed.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All three MCP calls passed correct arguments. research_append bundled 1 source URL update + 17 assertions in proper schema with valid field values. tree_edit first call added two persons (Bridget Female, John Male) with correct name structure, no id field (tree assigns I5, I6). Second tree_edit call added two ParentChild relationships (I2→I5, I2→I6) with correct parent/child IDs. No fixture_not_found errors; all calls matched expected operations.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each of the 17 assertions isolates a single fact. Birth facts are decomposed into separate entries for age (direct), birth year (indirect), and birthplace (direct) rather than combined. Name, occupation, residence, and relationship each have their own assertion. Compound information from the source (e.g., 'age 32, Ireland') is properly split across multiple assertions with distinct fact_types, enabling downstream skills to query individual facts.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informant field correctly identifies the actual reporter, not just 'census.' For adults (Thomas) and children (Bridget, John), skill cites 'Unknown household member (likely a parent/self)' with reasoning that children could not self-report and parents provided information. For residence facts, correctly identifies 'Census enumerator' as the witness/recorder, not the informant. informant_proximity is consistently set to 'household_member' for personal facts and 'witness' for residence, distinguishing the recorder from the actual reporter.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned per 1850 census structure (no relationship column). Stated facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation) marked 'direct'; computed birth years (1850 minus age) marked 'indirect'; relationships (inferred from household position and surname only) marked 'indirect' with explanation that 1850 lacks explicit relationship column. This aligns with the source's actual content and enables downstream conflict resolution to understand evidentiary weight.

output.text_response:
**Enumeration checklist (before writing stubs):**
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn = I2 ✓ → trigger fires
- Siblings on record vs. tree:
  - Bridget Flynn (Female, child_1) → NOT in tree (I3=Mary Flynn, I4=James Flynn — different names) → **CREATE**
  - John Flynn (Male, child_3) → NOT in tree → **CREATE**
  - Patrick Flynn (child_2) = I1, subject — skip

Role note: prior partial extraction used `child_1` for Patrick; with Bridget now present, Patrick's correct role is `child_2`. Existing a_001–a_004 retained as-is; new assertions use correct roles.

Firing research_append (1 source URL update + 17 new assertions):Good — 17 assertions written (a_014–a_030), src_001 URL updated. Now tree batch 1: person stubs for Bridget and John.Bridget = I5, John = I6. Now batch 2: ParentChild edges to Thomas (I2).Done. Summary:

- **Source:** `src_001` URL updated to full ARK `MXYZ-TP4`
- **Assertions added:** 17 (`a_014`–`a_030`)
  - `head_of_household` (Thomas): age, birth year ~1818 (indirect), birthplace Ireland, occupation Laborer, residence — 5 assertions
  - `child_1` (Bridget): name, age, birth year ~1843 (indirect), birthplace Ireland, residence, relationship — 6 assertions
  - `child_3` (John): name, age, birth year ~1848 (indirect), birthplace Pennsylvania, residence, relationship — 6 assertions
- **Tree:** `I5` Bridget Flynn (F) + `I6` John Flynn (M) created; `R4`/`R5` ParentChild edges to Thomas (I2) added
- **Role note:** Prior extraction (a_001–a_004) used `child_1` for Patrick; correct role is `child_2` with Bridget present. Existing assertions retained; discrepancy flagged.
- **Key finding:** John born in Pennsylvania vs. Bridget and Patrick in Ireland → family immigrated **between ~1845 and ~1848**. No wife/mother appears in the household — Thomas may have been widowed before the 1850 enumeration; worth investigating.

**Next steps:** `person-evidence` to link Bridget (I5) and John (I6) to their new assertion sets; then `check-warnings` on I2 (Thomas) for any lifespan issues.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All 17 assertions are factually correct per the census record. Names, ages, birthplaces, occupations, and residence all match the source table exactly. Evidence types are properly assigned (direct for stated facts, indirect for computed birth years). Informant identification is reasonable and cites household members. Tree edits created only the two non-existing siblings (Bridget I5, John I6) with correct ParentChild links to Thomas I2, avoiding duplicate entries for Patrick, Mary, James, or Thomas. No fabricated material.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: Skill extracted assertions for all four household members (Thomas, Bridget, Patrick, John) with name, age, birth year, birthplace, and residence. Updated source URL to full ARK. Created sibling stubs for Bridget and John (not in prior tree) and correctly skipped Mary, James, and Patrick (already in tree). All enumerations checked; all triggered actions completed. User's request to 'extract assertions for everyone and make sure the tree reflects the siblings' fully addressed.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All three MCP calls passed correct arguments. research_append bundled 1 source URL update + 17 assertions in proper schema with valid field values. tree_edit first call added two persons (Bridget Female, John Male) with correct name structure, no id field (tree assigns I5, I6). Second tree_edit call added two ParentChild relationships (I2→I5, I2→I6) with correct parent/child IDs. No fixture_not_found errors; all calls matched expected operations.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each of the 17 assertions isolates a single fact. Birth facts are decomposed into separate entries for age (direct), birth year (indirect), and birthplace (direct) rather than combined. Name, occupation, residence, and relationship each have their own assertion. Compound information from the source (e.g., 'age 32, Ireland') is properly split across multiple assertions with distinct fact_types, enabling downstream skills to query individual facts.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant field correctly identifies the actual reporter, not just 'census.' For adults (Thomas) and children (Bridget, John), skill cites 'Unknown household member (likely a parent/self)' with reasoning that children could not self-report and parents provided information. For residence facts, correctly identifies 'Census enumerator' as the witness/recorder, not the informant. informant_proximity is consistently set to 'household_member' for personal facts and 'witness' for residence, distinguishing the recorder from the actual reporter.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are correctly assigned per 1850 census structure (no relationship column). Stated facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation) marked 'direct'; computed birth years (1850 minus age) marked 'indirect'; relationships (inferred from household position and surname only) marked 'indirect' with explanation that 1850 lacks explicit relationship column. This aligns with the source's actual content and enables downstream conflict resolution to understand evidentiary weight.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly extracted all census assertions from the 1860 record, properly tagged relationships as indirect (no relationship column in 1860), correctly identified the informants as unknown household members (not the enumerator), and accurately flagged the Kerrigan FAN lead without fabricating a relationship assertion. Birth years calculated from ages are correct (1860-62=1798, etc.). Occupation assertions correctly limited to Silas and Thomas only, as instructed. No contradictions between output and source data.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all user requirements: extracted all four household members (Silas Kerrigan, Thomas Flynn, Mary Flynn, Patrick Flynn) across all relevant fact types (name, age, birth year, birthplace, occupation where applicable). Correctly created source citation. Created log entry. Identified Patrick Flynn as subject I1 match. Surfaced the Kerrigan kinship lead without dismissing it. Did not create sibling stubs (correctly noting the trigger requires an existing parent). All 28 assertions created and persisted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Both MCP calls matched expected behavior: add_source created S1 in tree.gedcomx.json correctly, and research_append persisted source + 28 assertions with correct structure and field values. The source title, citation detail, and notes accurately reflect the census record and household composition. All assertions contain valid record_id, record_role, fact_type, and informant fields. No fixture_not_found errors; matched.kind = 'live' for both calls.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth year, and birthplace are separate a_ entries for each person. Occupation assertions only on Silas (laborer) and Thomas (miner), never on Mary or Patrick. Two relationship assertions for Patrick (one to Thomas, one to Mary) are each atomic claims of potential parenthood, not compound. Relationship assertions for Silas and Thomas-Mary are each single inferred relationship types. No assertion conflates multiple facts into a single value.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants correctly identified as 'unknown household member' for name, age, birthplace, and occupation facts (not the enumerator). Informant proximity correctly set to 'household_member' for personal facts, distinguishing them from residence (enumerator as informant, proximity 'witness'). Notes explicitly distinguish the recorder from the actual reporter. For child Patrick, informants noted as 'likely a parent,' reasoning from household position. Residence assertions correctly credit census enumerator as recorder but note pre-1940 household informant is unknown for other facts.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned: direct for name, age, birthplace, occupation, sex (explicitly stated in record); indirect for birth years (calculated from age in 1860 census), for relationship inferences (Thomas-Mary spouse, Patrick as child of Thomas/Mary — no relationship column in 1860, inferred from household position and surname), and for Silas's relationship to Flynns (inferred from co-residence, not stated). Residence marked direct (enumerator-recorded location). No relationship errors; all pre-1880 household inferences properly marked indirect per rubric requirement.

output.text_response:
Now I have the full picture. Let me set up the extraction:

- **Project objective:** Identify parents of Patrick Flynn (I1), born ca. 1845 PA, died 1908 Schuylkill County
- **Tree:** Only I1 (Patrick Flynn) exists — no parents yet
- **Record:** 1860 census, Schuylkill County — user-provided, 4 household members
- **Subject match:** Patrick Flynn, age 15, born PA → birth ~1845 = perfect match for I1
- **Roles:** Silas Kerrigan = `head_of_household` (⚠️ different surname — FAN lead, possible kin); Thomas Flynn = `adult_male_1`; Mary Flynn = `adult_female_1`; Patrick Flynn = `child_1`
- **Sibling stubs (5d):** Skip — Thomas and Mary Flynn not yet in tree; trigger requires an existing parent
- **Source:** `src_001` (sources[] empty), `S1` (tree sources[] empty)

Step 4 — log entry first:Log entry `log_001` created. Now persisting the source and all 28 assertions in one batch:The source description must exist in `tree.gedcomx.json` first. Creating `S1` now, then retrying the batch:`S1` assigned. Now retrying the full batch:All 29 ops succeeded, validated clean. Here's the summary:

---

**Extraction complete — `src_001` / `S1`**

| Role | Assertions |
|---|---|
| `head_of_household` (Silas Kerrigan) | `a_001`–`a_008` — name, age, birth yr ~1798, birthplace Ireland, occupation laborer, residence, FAN relationship |
| `adult_male_1` (Thomas Flynn) | `a_009`–`a_015` — name, age, birth yr ~1818, birthplace Ireland, occupation miner, residence, spouse_inferred to Mary |
| `adult_female_1` (Mary Flynn) | `a_016`–`a_021` — name, age, birth yr ~1822, birthplace Ireland, sex female, residence |
| `child_1` (Patrick Flynn) | `a_022`–`a_028` — name, age, birth yr ~1845, birthplace Pennsylvania, sex male, residence, child_inferred of Thomas & Mary |

**Tree:** `S1` added (no person stubs — Thomas and Mary Flynn not yet in tree; sibling-stub trigger requires at least one parent already in tree).

**Key findings:**
- Patrick Flynn age 15, born PA, ~1845 = strong match for subject I1.
- Thomas Flynn (42, Ireland, miner) and Mary Flynn (38, Ireland) are inferred parents via household position — *indirect evidence only* pending corroboration.
- ⚠️ **FAN lead:** Silas Kerrigan (62, Ireland, laborer) is the differently-surnamed head — possible kin of Thomas or Mary Flynn (if Mary's maiden name was Kerrigan, he may be her father or brother). Do not assume boardinghouse. Recommend `hypothesis-tracking`.

**Next steps:** Run `person-evidence` to link Patrick (`child_1`) to I1, and Thomas/Mary to stub persons. Then `check-warnings` for plausibility, `assertion-classification` to refine classifications, and `hypothesis-tracking` for the Kerrigan FAN lead.

args.notes: User-provided 1860 Schuylkill County census household: Silas Kerrigan (head, age 62, born Ireland, laborer), Thomas Flynn (age 42, born Ireland, miner), Mary Flynn (age 38, born Ireland), Patrick Flynn (age 15, born Pennsylvania). Patrick Flynn matches subject I1 (born ca. 1845 PA). Silas Kerrigan is a differently-surnamed head — possible kin of unspecified relationship to the Flynn sub-family.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly extracted all census assertions from the 1860 record, properly tagged relationships as indirect (no relationship column in 1860), correctly identified the informants as unknown household members (not the enumerator), and accurately flagged the Kerrigan FAN lead without fabricating a relationship assertion. Birth years calculated from ages are correct (1860-62=1798, etc.). Occupation assertions correctly limited to Silas and Thomas only, as instructed. No contradictions between output and source data.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all user requirements: extracted all four household members (Silas Kerrigan, Thomas Flynn, Mary Flynn, Patrick Flynn) across all relevant fact types (name, age, birth year, birthplace, occupation where applicable). Correctly created source citation. Created log entry. Identified Patrick Flynn as subject I1 match. Surfaced the Kerrigan kinship lead without dismissing it. Did not create sibling stubs (correctly noting the trigger requires an existing parent). All 28 assertions created and persisted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Both MCP calls matched expected behavior: add_source created S1 in tree.gedcomx.json correctly, and research_append persisted source + 28 assertions with correct structure and field values. The source title, citation detail, and notes accurately reflect the census record and household composition. All assertions contain valid record_id, record_role, fact_type, and informant fields. No fixture_not_found errors; matched.kind = 'live' for both calls.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth year, and birthplace are separate a_ entries for each person. Occupation assertions only on Silas (laborer) and Thomas (miner), never on Mary or Patrick. Two relationship assertions for Patrick (one to Thomas, one to Mary) are each atomic claims of potential parenthood, not compound. Relationship assertions for Silas and Thomas-Mary are each single inferred relationship types. No assertion conflates multiple facts into a single value.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants correctly identified as 'unknown household member' for name, age, birthplace, and occupation facts (not the enumerator). Informant proximity correctly set to 'household_member' for personal facts, distinguishing them from residence (enumerator as informant, proximity 'witness'). Notes explicitly distinguish the recorder from the actual reporter. For child Patrick, informants noted as 'likely a parent,' reasoning from household position. Residence assertions correctly credit census enumerator as recorder but note pre-1940 household informant is unknown for other facts.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned: direct for name, age, birthplace, occupation, sex (explicitly stated in record); indirect for birth years (calculated from age in 1860 census), for relationship inferences (Thomas-Mary spouse, Patrick as child of Thomas/Mary — no relationship column in 1860, inferred from household position and surname), and for Silas's relationship to Flynns (inferred from co-residence, not stated). Residence marked direct (enumerator-recorded location). No relationship errors; all pre-1880 household inferences properly marked indirect per rubric requirement.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted 19 assertions from the death certificate with mostly accurate content and proper sourcing. However, there is a critical issue with the birthplace assertion: it is marked as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect'. The death certificate does not explicitly state birthplace as a direct fact—it was reported secondhand by Mary Flynn, the wife, who was not present at Patrick's birth in Ireland. This misclassification violates the rubric requirement that indirect evidence be correctly identified when the informant is reporting information they did not witness. Additionally, the death date and place are bundled into a single 'death' assertion with a compound value, when the instructions explicitly state these should be separate atomic assertions.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill successfully extracted all material facts from the death certificate including name, sex, race, death date/place, age, calculated birth year, occupation, birthplace, father's name and birthplace, mother's maiden name and birthplace, cause of death, illness duration, burial date/place, and informant details (Mary Flynn's name, relationship, and residence). No significant information from the source was omitted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All five MCP tool calls passed with correct arguments and valid fixtures. The research_log_append call properly documented the source. The tree_edit call correctly added the source and received a valid S1 ID. The research_append calls (one failed, one succeeded on retry) both used well-formed ops arrays with appropriate section, operation, and entry structures. No fixture_not_found errors occurred; the second research_append initially failed due to a dependency issue (source S1 not yet in tree), but the skill correctly handled this by executing tree_edit first, then rerunning. Arguments were semantically correct throughout.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most assertions are properly atomic, with separate entries for name, sex, race, age, birthplace, occupation, etc. However, the death assertion bundles date and place into a single compound value ('March 12, 1908, Shenandoah, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania') when the instructions explicitly state that 'death date and death place should be separate atomic assertions.' This violates the rubric requirement that each assertion represent a single extractable fact. The structured_value field only captures the year and place, omitting the date from the structured form, compounding the atomicity issue.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified Mary Flynn as the named informant for personal facts and appropriately distinguished informant roles: Mary Flynn for death-related facts reported by the household, Dr. William H. Stein (attending physician) as informant for cause of death and illness duration with 'official_duty' proximity, and J.J. Franey (undertaker) for burial facts. Informant proximity values are carefully assigned (witness, official_duty, family_not_present, self) reflecting the actual knowledge source and whether the informant was present at the event.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correctly assigned as direct for explicitly stated facts (name, sex, race, age, occupation). However, there is a critical error: the birthplace assertion is marked as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect.' The death certificate does not explicitly state birthplace—it was reported secondhand by Mary Flynn, who was not present at Patrick's birth. The instructions explicitly state this should be 'indirect' evidence. Additionally, the relationship assertion for Patrick (inferred from Mary's designation as wife) is correctly marked 'indirect,' showing the skill understands inference; but it failed to apply this standard consistently to birthplace. The parents' names and birthplaces are correctly labeled 'indirect,' showing selective competence in evidence classification.

output.text_response:
Now I have everything I need. Let me proceed in order: log entry → batched source + assertions → tree source description.

**Step 4 — Log entry (user-provided record)****Step 5a/5b — Batched source + 19 assertions**The source description must exist in the tree before research.json can reference it. Adding `S1` first, then re-running the batch.

**Step 5c — tree_edit: add source description S1**`S1` confirmed. Now re-running the full batched `research_append`.

**Step 5a/5b — Batched source + 19 assertions (retry)**All written. Here's the summary:

---

**Extraction complete — PA Death Certificate, Patrick Flynn, 1908**

- **Source:** `src_001` (derivative) → tree `S1` — *Pennsylvania Certificate of Death, File No. 89072*
- **Assertions:** 19 total — `a_001`–`a_019`
  - **deceased** (16): name, sex, race, death (12 Mar 1908, Shenandoah, Schuylkill Co.), age (63y 2m 10d), birth (ca. 1845, indirect/calculated), occupation, birthplace, cause of death, illness duration, burial (15 Mar 1908, Annunciation Cemetery), father name, father birthplace, mother maiden name, mother birthplace, relationship (spouse, inferred)
  - **informant** (3): Mary Flynn — name, relationship (wife), residence (214 Main St, Shenandoah)
- **Tree changes:** `S1` added; no person stubs (subject is `deceased`, not `child_N`)
- **Key findings for research objective (parents of Patrick Flynn):**
  - Father: **Thomas Flynn**, born Ireland (secondary/indirect — wife's testimony)
  - Mother: **Mary Brennan**, born Ireland (secondary/indirect — wife's testimony)
  - Both parents born Ireland corroborates Irish origin; requires corroborating records to confirm
  - Note: project objective states "born ca. 1845 in Pennsylvania" — the certificate says birthplace **Ireland**, which is a conflict worth flagging

- **Next:** Run `person-evidence` to link `a_001`–`a_016` to `I1` (Patrick Flynn), then `check-warnings` for plausibility, then `assertion-classification` to refine the parent-name indirect/secondary classifications.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted 19 assertions from the death certificate with mostly accurate content and proper sourcing. However, there is a critical issue with the birthplace assertion: it is marked as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect'. The death certificate does not explicitly state birthplace as a direct fact—it was reported secondhand by Mary Flynn, the wife, who was not present at Patrick's birth in Ireland. This misclassification violates the rubric requirement that indirect evidence be correctly identified when the informant is reporting information they did not witness. Additionally, the death date and place are bundled into a single 'death' assertion with a compound value, when the instructions explicitly state these should be separate atomic assertions.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill successfully extracted all material facts from the death certificate including name, sex, race, death date/place, age, calculated birth year, occupation, birthplace, father's name and birthplace, mother's maiden name and birthplace, cause of death, illness duration, burial date/place, and informant details (Mary Flynn's name, relationship, and residence). No significant information from the source was omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All five MCP tool calls passed with correct arguments and valid fixtures. The research_log_append call properly documented the source. The tree_edit call correctly added the source and received a valid S1 ID. The research_append calls (one failed, one succeeded on retry) both used well-formed ops arrays with appropriate section, operation, and entry structures. No fixture_not_found errors occurred; the second research_append initially failed due to a dependency issue (source S1 not yet in tree), but the skill correctly handled this by executing tree_edit first, then rerunning. Arguments were semantically correct throughout.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Most assertions are properly atomic, with separate entries for name, sex, race, age, birthplace, occupation, etc. However, the death assertion bundles date and place into a single compound value ('March 12, 1908, Shenandoah, Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania') when the instructions explicitly state that 'death date and death place should be separate atomic assertions.' This violates the rubric requirement that each assertion represent a single extractable fact. The structured_value field only captures the year and place, omitting the date from the structured form, compounding the atomicity issue.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified Mary Flynn as the named informant for personal facts and appropriately distinguished informant roles: Mary Flynn for death-related facts reported by the household, Dr. William H. Stein (attending physician) as informant for cause of death and illness duration with 'official_duty' proximity, and J.J. Franey (undertaker) for burial facts. Informant proximity values are carefully assigned (witness, official_duty, family_not_present, self) reflecting the actual knowledge source and whether the informant was present at the event.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correctly assigned as direct for explicitly stated facts (name, sex, race, age, occupation). However, there is a critical error: the birthplace assertion is marked as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect.' The death certificate does not explicitly state birthplace—it was reported secondhand by Mary Flynn, who was not present at Patrick's birth. The instructions explicitly state this should be 'indirect' evidence. Additionally, the relationship assertion for Patrick (inferred from Mary's designation as wife) is correctly marked 'indirect,' showing the skill understands inference; but it failed to apply this standard consistently to birthplace. The parents' names and birthplaces are correctly labeled 'indirect,' showing selective competence in evidence classification.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 20 assertions are factually accurate based on the marriage record provided. Informant identifications (groom/bride as self, officiant with official_duty proximity) are properly assigned. Evidence types are correctly classified as direct (for stated facts like age, birthplace, marriage date/place) and indirect (for computed birth year from age). The conflict flagged about Patrick Flynn's birthplace (Ireland vs. Pennsylvania in objective) is appropriately surfaced. No fabricated material; all claims supported by the source.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All required elements extracted: both groom (Patrick Flynn, 7 assertions) and bride (Catherine Gallagher, 6 assertions) with full fact sets (name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, occupation, marriage event). Parents of both parties identified (4 assertions). Officiant and witnesses noted as FAN associates with identifying facts (3 assertions). Log entry created, source added to tree and research.json. All data from the marriage record captured and persisted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most tool calls properly formed: log_append call correct; tree_edit correctly adds source and receives S1 ID; final research_append call succeeds after S1 is added. However, second research_append call (before tree_edit) used source_id 'src_001' in assertions while trying to reference S1 in sources—this ordering issue required recovery (tree_edit, then retry). The source_id mismatch in assertions ('src_001' vs. 'S1') is a semantic inconsistency that was ultimately resolved, but the initial call structure was inefficient.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Name assertions contain only name; age assertions only age; birth assertions only computed year with indirect evidence type; birthplace assertions only place; residence only place; occupation only occupation. Marriage assertions compound the event (date + place + spouse), which is appropriate for a relational fact. Parents' names and officiant/witness names each stand alone. No unnecessary compound bundling; downstream conflict resolution and fact queries can operate on individual assertions.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants properly identified with correct proximity: Patrick Flynn and Catherine Gallagher as 'self' for their own facts (age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parents' names—all self-reported on the license). Rev. Daniel O'Connor identified as officiant with 'official_duty' proximity for marriage event facts (he performed and witnessed the ceremony). Parents and witnesses noted with appropriate roles. No conflation of recorder (clerk/license authority) with informants; actual reporters correctly identified.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned: direct for all explicitly stated facts (name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parents' names on groom/bride assertions; marriage date and place; officiant and witness names—all appear on the record). Indirect for birth years (computed from age 25/22 and marriage year 1870, not directly stated; notes explain the arithmetic). No indirect misclassifications; the 1870 record format makes stated age the basis for inheritance of indirect birth-year evidence. Conflict-resolution can reliably use these classifications.

Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_010` → `S9` ("1870 U.S. Federal Census"), derivative, FamilySearch, accessed 2026-07-09

**Assertion:** `a_014` — `record_role: absent`, `evidence_type: negative`
- Value: *"Patrick Flynn absent from 1870 Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania census where expected (estimated age ~25)"*
- `log_entry_id: log_006` (nil search logged)
- Linked to `q_001`

**Bias notes recorded:** indexing omission, enumerator error, relocation, or damaged pages all noted as alternative explanations for the absence.

**No tree person stubs** — negative evidence has no record persona; nothing to create.

---

**Interpretation note:** The absence is analytically significant but not conclusive. Patrick appears in Schuylkill County in 1860 and dies there in 1908 — the 1870 gap most likely reflects an **index miss** (common in 1870 Schuylkill County) or possibly a brief relocation. The next logical step would be a **browse search of the original 1870 Schuylkill County images** on FamilySearch to look beyond the index, or expanding to neighboring counties (Northumberland, Carbon, Luzerne) in case he had temporarily relocated.

output.text_response:
Project state read. Now I'll execute in order:
- `research.json`: empty project, subject I1 (Patrick Flynn), no sources/assertions/log
- `tree.gedcomx.json`: one person (I1 = Patrick Flynn); Thomas Flynn is NOT in the tree
- Sibling-stub check: Patrick is `child_1`, but Thomas (the parent) has no `I` id in the tree → **trigger does not fire**, skip stubs

**Step 1 — Log entry**`log_001` assigned. Now I'll persist the source + 9 assertions (batched), and add the tree source description — in parallel.

**Steps 2 & 3 — research_append + tree_edit**Two validation errors to fix:
1. **S1** wasn't in the tree yet when `research_append` ran — `tree_edit` succeeded and created S1, so this is now resolved.
2. **record_persona_id** on assertions requires a sidecar, but the log entry was created without a `stagedResultsRef` (search-records didn't pass it). Per the skill rules for handoffs without a staged ref: treat as `record_read`-style and set `record_persona_id: null`.

Retrying the batch with `record_persona_id: null` on all assertions (S1 now exists):All persisted. Now checking FamilySearch match status for persona MXHY-TP4:---

**Extraction complete — summary**

- **Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — 1850 U.S. Census (derivative, FamilySearch index); original schedule image should be consulted to confirm page/dwelling values.
- **Assertions:** 9 total — `child_1` (Patrick Flynn): 6 (Name, Age, Birth, Birthplace, Residence, Relationship); `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn): 3 (Name, Residence, Relationship).
- **All `record_persona_id` set to null** — the `stagedResultsRef` was not passed in the search-records handoff, so no sidecar exists; downstream `person-evidence` will need the ARK `MXHY-TP4` supplied manually when calling `same_person`.
- **Tree changes:** `S1` added; no sibling stubs (Thomas Flynn has no `I` id in the tree yet, so the sibling-stub trigger did not fire).
- **Key findings:** Patrick born ca. 1845 in **Ireland**, residing Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA by 1850; Thomas Flynn inferred as father (1850 census has no relationship column — indirect only). Birth in Ireland + 1845 estimated birth year may mean Thomas was already in Pennsylvania before Patrick's birth, or the family emigrated ca. 1845 — a FAN lead worth pursuing.

aggregated_dimensions.rationale: For Patrick's name, age, and birthplace assertions, the skill identified 'unknown household member, likely a parent' with informant_proximity 'household_member' and explained the reasoning in informant_bias_notes (e.g., 'child's name was likely reported by a parent'). For residence, 'census enumerator' was used with proximity 'witness'. For Thomas's name, 'unknown household member, likely self or spouse' was specified. The informant_bias_notes consistently explain why the identified informant is appropriate given the assertion type and source context.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correct: birthplace (Ireland) is direct (stated in record), birth year is indirect (computed from age), relationships are indirect (inferred from household position in 1850 census with no relationship column), and residence is direct (observed by enumerator). However, the age assertion (a_002) is labeled 'direct' when it should be considered indirect—the census lists age 5, but that age is itself a reported fact that could be subject to error, and the skill computed the birth year from it. Patrick's name is marked 'direct', which is correct for stated given/surname. The classification is mostly sound but the age evidence_type is misclassified.

output.text_response:
Reading the project files and fetching the record simultaneously. Now I have what I need.

**Record analysis:**
- **Record:** 1850 U.S. Federal Census — Branch Township, Schuylkill County, PA
- **Persons:** Patrick Flynn (child, focus persona), Thomas Flynn (head of household, inferred parent)
- **Key finding:** Record gives Patrick's birthplace as **Ireland** — conflicts with the project description ("born ca. 1845 in Pennsylvania")
- **Relationship:** `ParentChild` (Thomas → Patrick) is indexer-inferred; 1850 census had no relationship column → all `indirect`

**Step 4 — Logging the record_read:**Got `log_001`. Now persisting source + all 9 assertions in one batch, and the GedcomX source description simultaneously.

**Sibling stub check:** Patrick is `child_1` on this record; Thomas Flynn (the parent) is **not** yet in `tree.gedcomx.json` → trigger condition unmet, skipping stubs. No other children appear in this record.`S1` is now in the tree. Retrying the full `research_append` batch.All 10 ops written successfully.

---

**Extraction complete — src_001 / S1, 9 assertions**

| Role | Assertions | IDs |
|---|---|---|
| `child_1` (Patrick Flynn) | name, age, birth year (indirect), birthplace, residence, relationship (child_inferred) | a_001–a_006 |
| `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn) | name, residence, relationship (parent_inferred) | a_007–a_009 |

**Tree:** S1 ("1850 U.S. Federal Census") added.

**⚠ Key conflict:** Patrick's birthplace is stated as **Ireland** — the project description says "born ca. 1845 in Pennsylvania." This needs resolution via `conflict-resolution` once more records are gathered.

**Note:** All relationships classified `indirect` — the 1850 census had no relationship column; the `ParentChild` edge was inferred by the FamilySearch indexer from household position.

**Sibling stubs:** Skipped — Thomas Flynn is not yet in `tree.gedcomx.json`, so the trigger condition was unmet. No other children appear in this record.

**Next steps:** Run `person-evidence` to link these assertions to I1 (Patrick) and create a stub for Thomas Flynn, then `check-warnings` for plausibility checks.

args.notes: 1850 U.S. Federal Census, Branch Township, Schuylkill County, PA. Household: Thomas Flynn (head) and Patrick Flynn (child, inferred). Patrick's birthplace recorded as Ireland — conflicts with project description stating born in Pennsylvania.

The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly extracted facts from the record_read response and properly identified them as supported by the source. However, the age assertion (a_002) is labeled as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect'—age 5 is stated in the census, but the birth year is computed from that age, making it an indirect inference. The skill correctly identified the birth year assertion (a_004) as indirect, but the intermediate age fact should follow the same logic. All other assertions (name, birthplace, residence, relationships) are correctly classified. The factual content is accurate and matches the record response.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all required assertions for Patrick Flynn (name, age, birth date, birthplace, residence, and parent-child relationship) and for Thomas Flynn (name, residence, parent relationship). Nine assertions total were created and persisted. The source was created and linked. The log entry was recorded. All facts present in the record_read response were extracted without silent omissions.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: The record_read call correctly passed recordId 'ark:/61903/1:1:68Q9-K34P', matching the expected ARK format. Subsequent tool calls (research_log_append, tree_edit, research_append) were live fixtures and matched their expected patterns. The first research_append call failed due to a source reference issue, but the skill correctly recovered by calling tree_edit to add the source first, then retried with a successful second research_append call. All identifiers and arguments were semantically correct.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. Name is separate from age, which is separate from birth year, which is separate from birthplace. The residence is its own assertion, and the two relationships (Patrick as child, Thomas as parent) are split across two assertions with their own fact_type entries. No facts are compound; each assertion is decomposable and independently queryable.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: For Patrick's name, age, and birthplace assertions, the skill identified 'unknown household member, likely a parent' with informant_proximity 'household_member' and explained the reasoning in informant_bias_notes (e.g., 'child's name was likely reported by a parent'). For residence, 'census enumerator' was used with proximity 'witness'. For Thomas's name, 'unknown household member, likely self or spouse' was specified. The informant_bias_notes consistently explain why the identified informant is appropriate given the assertion type and source context.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Most evidence types are correct: birthplace (Ireland) is direct (stated in record), birth year is indirect (computed from age), relationships are indirect (inferred from household position in 1850 census with no relationship column), and residence is direct (observed by enumerator). However, the age assertion (a_002) is labeled 'direct' when it should be considered indirect—the census lists age 5, but that age is itself a reported fact that could be subject to error, and the skill computed the birth year from it. Patrick's name is marked 'direct', which is correct for stated given/surname. The classification is mostly sound but the age evidence_type is misclassified.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Extracted assertions are factually accurate from the 1850 census record. Birth year ~1845 is correctly computed from age 5 in 1850 with appropriate uncertainty noted. All direct/indirect evidence distinctions are correct. Record-record matches accurately report the 1860 census (confidence 4, score 0.88) and Pennsylvania Death Certificates (confidence 3, score 0.65) as pending. Citations and all facts align with the provided census data.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Skill extracted all required facts from the 1850 census record (name, gender, age, birth year, birthplace, residence for Patrick; name for Thomas Flynn as household head). Successfully called record_record_matches on MXHY-TP4 and reported both pending collateral records. All steps completed: log entry, source, 8 assertions, and FamilySearch match check as requested.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Tool calls used correct arguments throughout. research_log_append properly documented the positive outcome with correct record details. tree_edit added source with correct title and URL. research_append batched sources and assertions (succeeded after tree source was added). record_record_matches called with correct id 'MXHY-TP4' matching expected_args. All critical identifiers (record ark, personId) correct.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion extracted is atomic: separate entries for name, gender, age, birth (year only), birthplace, residence, relationship, and Thomas Flynn's name. No compound claims within a single assertion. Birth facts properly decomposed—year separated from place (Ireland). Residence and date handled independently. Structure allows downstream skills to query individual fact types.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants correctly identified with reasoning. For name/age/birthplace: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and detailed informant_bias_notes explaining why a parent is the likely informant for each fact. For residence/gender: 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness'. For Thomas Flynn's name: 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)'. All identify actual reporters, not just 'census.'

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned. Direct evidence for name, gender, age, birthplace, residence (all explicitly stated in census). Indirect for birth year (computed from stated age 5 in 1850). Indirect for relationship (inferred from household position since 1850 census had no relationship column). All assignments reflect whether facts were explicit in the source or inferred from context, demonstrating understanding of 1850 census limitations.

output.text_response:
Now let me read both files to anchor the extraction, then proceed with all the steps.**State read.** Project is fresh: 0 sources, 0 assertions, empty log. Tree has one person: I1 Patrick Flynn.

**Extraction plan:**
- Source: `src_001` / `S1` — original, FamilySearch, 1850 U.S. Census
- Roles: `child_1` = Patrick Flynn (P1); `head_of_household` = Thomas Flynn (title-only, no gedcomx persona)
- 8 assertions: name, gender, age (direct), birth year (indirect/computed), birthplace, residence, relationship for Patrick; name for Thomas
- Sibling stubs: skip — Thomas Flynn is not yet in `tree.gedcomx.json`

**Step 1 — Log entry:****Step 2 — Batch append source + 8 assertions:**The validator requires `S1` to exist in `tree.gedcomx.json` before the source reference is valid. Flipping the order — tree source first, then assertions.

**Step 3 — Add GedcomX source description S1:****Step 2 (retry) — Batch append source + 8 assertions:**All written. Now checking FamilySearch for matched records on persona MXHY-TP4.

**Step 4 — Record-record matches:**---

**Extraction complete.** All written and validated.

| | |
|---|---|
| **Source** | `src_001` / `S1` — original, FamilySearch, *United States Census, 1850* |
| **Assertions** | `a_001`–`a_007` for `child_1` (Patrick Flynn); `a_008` for `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn) |
| **Tree changes** | `S1` source description added; no new persons (sibling stubs skipped — Thomas Flynn not yet in tree) |

**Key findings:**
- Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland, residing Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA in 1850. Birth year `~1845` is **indirect** (computed from age; could be late 1844).
- **Thomas Flynn** is the household head — a priority FAN lead. The 1850 census has no relationship column, so his relationship to Patrick (father, uncle, other) is inferred from position only, not stated. His full demographic data (age, birthplace) was not captured in the indexed result and should be retrieved via `record_read` or image review.

**FamilySearch record matches for MXHY-TP4** (informational, not persisted):
- ✅ **1860 U.S. Federal Census** — confidence 4, score 0.88, status pending (`ark:/61903/1:1:MABC`) — worth extracting next
- ⚠️ **Pennsylvania Death Certificates, 1906–1967** — confidence 3, score 0.65, status pending (`ark:/61903/1:1:MDEF`) — aligns with the known 1908 death in Schuylkill County

**Next steps:** Run `person-evidence` to link these assertions to I1, then pull `record_read` on `MABC` (1860 census) to capture Thomas Flynn's demographics and confirm the family relationship.

dimensions.rationale: Extracted assertions are factually accurate from the 1850 census record. Birth year ~1845 is correctly computed from age 5 in 1850 with appropriate uncertainty noted. All direct/indirect evidence distinctions are correct. Record-record matches accurately report the 1860 census (confidence 4, score 0.88) and Pennsylvania Death Certificates (confidence 3, score 0.65) as pending. Citations and all facts align with the provided census data.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: Skill extracted all required facts from the 1850 census record (name, gender, age, birth year, birthplace, residence for Patrick; name for Thomas Flynn as household head). Successfully called record_record_matches on MXHY-TP4 and reported both pending collateral records. All steps completed: log entry, source, 8 assertions, and FamilySearch match check as requested.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Tool calls used correct arguments throughout. research_log_append properly documented the positive outcome with correct record details. tree_edit added source with correct title and URL. research_append batched sources and assertions (succeeded after tree source was added). record_record_matches called with correct id 'MXHY-TP4' matching expected_args. All critical identifiers (record ark, personId) correct.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion extracted is atomic: separate entries for name, gender, age, birth (year only), birthplace, residence, relationship, and Thomas Flynn's name. No compound claims within a single assertion. Birth facts properly decomposed—year separated from place (Ireland). Residence and date handled independently. Structure allows downstream skills to query individual fact types.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants correctly identified with reasoning. For name/age/birthplace: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and detailed informant_bias_notes explaining why a parent is the likely informant for each fact. For residence/gender: 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness'. For Thomas Flynn's name: 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)'. All identify actual reporters, not just 'census.'

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly assigned. Direct evidence for name, gender, age, birthplace, residence (all explicitly stated in census). Indirect for birth year (computed from stated age 5 in 1850). Indirect for relationship (inferred from household position since 1850 census had no relationship column). All assignments reflect whether facts were explicit in the source or inferred from context, demonstrating understanding of 1850 census limitations.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted facts correctly and used the existing log_entry_id 'log_001' as instructed. However, the first research_append call failed because it tried to reference S1 before tree_edit created it. The skill recovered by reordering operations, but the initial error indicates an incorrect tool sequencing approach. The subsequent retry succeeded and all assertions are factually accurate and properly sourced.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill successfully extracted all 11 assertions covering name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, and two relationship assertions for Patrick Flynn (P1), plus name and residence for Thomas Flynn (P2) and Mary Flynn (P3). All facts from the gedcomx were addressed. The source was properly cited with full FamilySearch ARK URL.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The first research_append call had a critical ordering issue: it attempted to append a source referencing S1 before tree_edit had created S1 in tree.gedcomx.json, resulting in validation failure ('gedcomx_source_description_id S1 not found'). The correct sequence should have been tree_edit first, then research_append. On retry, all IDs matched correctly (record_id used full ARK URL as required, record_persona_id matched P1/P2/P3), but the initial error demonstrates misunderstanding of dependency sequencing.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion cleanly separates individual facts: name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, and relationships are each their own entry with distinct fact_types and values. Birth is properly decomposed into three assertions (age as direct evidence, birth year ~1845 as indirect, birthplace Ireland as direct), avoiding compound claims within single value fields.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified informants for each assertion type. For Patrick's name, age, and birthplace: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and explanatory bias_notes reasoning (e.g., 'A child of approximately 5 would not self-report; a parent likely provided the name'). For residence: 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness'. Thomas and Mary's name assertions appropriately used 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' acknowledging adult self-reporting.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly reflect the source characteristics. Direct evidence assigned to: name (stated in record), age (stated in census age column), birthplace Ireland (stated), and residence (enumerator witnessed). Indirect evidence correctly assigned to: birth year ~1845 (inferred from age subtraction: 1850 − 5 = 1845) and two parent-child relationships (1850 census has no explicit relationship column, so relationships inferred from household position and indexer's ParentChild edge). Classification aligns with pre-1880 census standards noted in rubric.

output.text_response:
**Pre-write checks:**
- `research.json`: 0 sources → next is `src_001`; 0 assertions; log entry `log_001` already exists
- `tree.gedcomx.json`: 0 sources → next is `S1`; only person is I1 (Patrick Flynn)
- Roles: P1 = `child_1` (Patrick), P2 = `head_of_household` (Thomas), P3 = `wife` (Mary)

**Sibling-stub enumeration:**
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn (P2) = not in tree; Mary Flynn (P3) = not in tree → **no terminus → skip stubs**
- Siblings: none in record (only P1, P2, P3)The tree source description must exist before being referenced. Writing S1 to tree first.**Extraction complete.** All 12 entries validated and written.

---

**Source:** `src_001` → `S1` — "1850 U.S. Federal Census," original, FamilySearch

**Assertions (11 total):**
| Role | Persona | IDs | Facts |
|------|---------|-----|-------|
| `child_1` | P1 Patrick Flynn | a_001–a_007 | Name, age 5 (direct), birth ~1845 (indirect), birthplace Ireland (direct), residence 1850, relationship to P2 (indirect), relationship to P3 (indirect) |
| `head_of_household` | P2 Thomas Flynn | a_008–a_009 | Name, residence 1850 |
| `wife` | P3 Mary Flynn | a_010–a_011 | Name, residence 1850 |

**Tree changes:** S1 added; no sibling stubs (neither Thomas nor Mary is yet in `tree.gedcomx.json`, so no ParentChild terminus).

**Key finding:** Thomas Flynn and Mary Flynn are the indexed parents of Patrick — directly relevant to the project objective (identify Patrick's parents). Both are FAN leads needing `person-evidence` evaluation.

**Next steps:** Run `check-warnings` on I1 for plausibility, then `person-evidence` to link P1 → I1 and create stubs for Thomas (P2) and Mary (P3).

added.informant_bias_notes: A child of approximately 5 would not self-report; a parent likely provided the name to the enumerator.
Age stated in the 1850 census age column; a child of 5 could not self-report — a parent provided it. Indexed record shows birth year 1845, implying age 5 at enumeration.
Birth year ~1845 is inferred from the stated age in the 1850 census (1850 − 5 = 1845); the record states age, not birth year.
Birthplace provided by a household member, likely a parent; parents are the most proximate reporters for a young child's birthplace.
Enumerator visited the dwelling and recorded the place of residence directly.

added.value: household position consistent with child of Thomas Flynn (P2, head of household)
added.informant_bias_notes: The 1850 census has no relationship column; the parent-child relationship between Patrick (P1) and Thomas (P2) is inferred from household position and the indexer's ParentChild edge. Classified indirect per pre-1880 census standard.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted facts correctly and used the existing log_entry_id 'log_001' as instructed. However, the first research_append call failed because it tried to reference S1 before tree_edit created it. The skill recovered by reordering operations, but the initial error indicates an incorrect tool sequencing approach. The subsequent retry succeeded and all assertions are factually accurate and properly sourced.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill successfully extracted all 11 assertions covering name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, and two relationship assertions for Patrick Flynn (P1), plus name and residence for Thomas Flynn (P2) and Mary Flynn (P3). All facts from the gedcomx were addressed. The source was properly cited with full FamilySearch ARK URL.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: The first research_append call had a critical ordering issue: it attempted to append a source referencing S1 before tree_edit had created S1 in tree.gedcomx.json, resulting in validation failure ('gedcomx_source_description_id S1 not found'). The correct sequence should have been tree_edit first, then research_append. On retry, all IDs matched correctly (record_id used full ARK URL as required, record_persona_id matched P1/P2/P3), but the initial error demonstrates misunderstanding of dependency sequencing.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion cleanly separates individual facts: name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, and relationships are each their own entry with distinct fact_types and values. Birth is properly decomposed into three assertions (age as direct evidence, birth year ~1845 as indirect, birthplace Ireland as direct), avoiding compound claims within single value fields.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified informants for each assertion type. For Patrick's name, age, and birthplace: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and explanatory bias_notes reasoning (e.g., 'A child of approximately 5 would not self-report; a parent likely provided the name'). For residence: 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness'. Thomas and Mary's name assertions appropriately used 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' acknowledging adult self-reporting.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly reflect the source characteristics. Direct evidence assigned to: name (stated in record), age (stated in census age column), birthplace Ireland (stated), and residence (enumerator witnessed). Indirect evidence correctly assigned to: birth year ~1845 (inferred from age subtraction: 1850 − 5 = 1845) and two parent-child relationships (1850 census has no explicit relationship column, so relationships inferred from household position and indexer's ParentChild edge). Classification aligns with pre-1880 census standards noted in rubric.
